<par>The first syllables of the Dali and Jianchuan compounds are undoubtedly loans from Chinese <hanform>嘴</hanform> (Mand. <latinform>zuǐ</latinform>).</par>
<par>Borrowed from Chinese <latinform>bózi</latinform>, but the Chinese is ultimately from etymon 484 *b(r/w)a-n/t THROAT.</par>

<par>The second syllable of the Bai (Bijiang) form <latinform>vĩ³³ ɕui³³</latinform> is an obvious loan from Chinese (cf. Mand. <latinform>shuǐ</latinform>), although the other Bai forms listed seem to reflect the present etymon.</par>

<par>The last syllable has been tagged 1276 by analogy with the Tujia forms for ‘chest’ <latinform>li⁵⁵ kʰo⁵⁵ tsʰũ⁵⁵</latinform> (43384,127201) and <latinform>li⁵⁵kho⁵⁵tshoŋ⁵⁵</latinform> (193110), since the second syllables of the Bai and Tujia forms seem to match up as well.</par>
<par><emph>remove division</emph>. b from Ch? If so: what about <latinform>pi</latinform> etc. above</par>
